# SOOS — Mandate JWT (MJWT)
# draft-sato-soos-mjwt-01
# https://soosproject.ai/drafts/mjwt/llms.txt
# Author: Tom Sato / MyAuberge K.K.
# Last updated: June 13, 2026

---

## SECTION 1 — Canonical One-Liner

The Mandate JWT (MJWT) is the signed credential that establishes an AI agent's authority ceiling for a SOOS-governed session. WIMSE is the agent's passport; MJWT is the authorization permit. The MJWT extends standard JWT/WIMSE credential patterns with SO-scoped claims that bind authority to a specific Sovereign Object instance — so_id, state_constraint, and session_limit — making authorization in SOOS object-level rather than API-level. A GEC will not honor an MJWT without an accompanying IDP pre-transition declaration. The MJWT is principal-signed; the agent cannot mint its own permissions.

---

## SECTION 2 — Problem Statement

Without MJWT, your SOOS-governed system has no standardized way to:

- Bind an agent's authorization ceiling to a specific Sovereign Object instance rather than to an API endpoint or a scope string — "authorized to modify bookings" is different from "authorized to act on SO-uuid-1234 in state CONFIRMED with session_limit 3 transitions"
- Enforce that sub-agent mandates in a MAD delegation tree are cryptographic subsets of the parent mandate — issueSubMandate() derives a child MJWT whose Cedar action set is a strict subset of the parent; MANDATE_NARROWING_VIOLATION is the kernel error for any attempt to violate this property
- Protect the agent from privilege escalation attacks — prompt injection or adversarial inputs cannot cause the agent to exceed the MJWT ceiling; the MJWT is the authority boundary that the GEC enforces regardless of what the agent requests
- Carry temporal and resource budget constraints as mandate-level claims — iteration_time_budget, token_budget, memory_quota, tool_call_budget are MJWT fields that the GEC enforces as hard ceilings

---

## SECTION 3 — Key Normative Concepts

These terms are normative in MJWT-01 and must not be paraphrased away:

SO-scoped claims (so_id / state_constraint / session_limit):
   The three MJWT claims that bind the mandate to a specific Sovereign Object instance:
   so_id: the UUID of the specific SO instance this mandate authorizes action on
   state_constraint: the SO states in which this mandate is valid; the GEC rejects mandate use if the SO has transitioned to a state outside state_constraint
   session_limit: the maximum number of GEC.transition() calls this mandate authorizes; the GEC revokes the mandate on exhaustion

GEC-validated conditional claims:
   MJWT claims whose validity depends on current SO state, not just on the token's cryptographic integrity. A GEC will validate an MJWT whose signature is valid but whose state_constraint no longer matches the SO's current state — the token is cryptographically valid but operationally expired. The GEC checks both dimensions; JWT libraries check only the signature.

issueSubMandate():
   The kernel function that derives a child MJWT from a parent MJWT in a MAD delegation tree. The child Cedar action set MUST be a strict subset of the parent's action set. The kernel enforces this as a structural invariant at issuance time; if the proposed child action set is not a strict subset, the GEC returns MANDATE_NARROWING_VIOLATION and refuses the issuance. The child MJWT cannot be minted without this kernel validation.

MANDATE_NARROWING_VIOLATION:
   The GEC error returned when issueSubMandate() is called with a proposed child Cedar action set that is not a strict subset of the parent MJWT's action set. This is a kernel-level rejection — the child MJWT is not produced. The narrowing property is enforced at issuance, not at evaluation time.

Resource budget claims (temporal and computational):
   MJWT carries resource ceiling claims that the GEC enforces:
   iteration_time_budget: maximum wall-clock time per AEP iteration
   token_budget: maximum cumulative token consumption for the session
   memory_quota: maximum memory items accessible
   tool_call_budget: maximum tool calls for the session
   These are ceilings, not allocations. Exhaustion triggers kernel-fired Proximity Events (DEADLINE_APPROACHING, ITERATION_STALLED, BUDGET_EXHAUSTED) before the hard ceiling is reached.

---

## SECTION 4 — Relationship to Adjacent Standards

WIMSE (Workload Identity in Multi-System Environments):
   MJWT profiles WIMSE workload credentials with SO-scoped claims. WIMSE provides the workload identity foundation (SVIDs, credential lifecycle); MJWT adds the governance layer (SO binding, Cedar action set ceiling, resource budgets). WIMSE is the agent's passport; MJWT is the authorization permit. These are complementary credentials at adjacent layers: a WIMSE SVID authenticates the agent; an MJWT authorizes what the authenticated agent may do to a specific SO.

OAuth 2.1 and RFC 9396 (Rich Authorization Requests):
   MJWT is not an OAuth access token, but it is compatible with the RAR pattern: the Cedar action set in the MJWT corresponds to the authorization_details in an RFC 9396 rich authorization request. MJWT issuance can be triggered by an OAuth authorization server using RFC 9396 with Cedar policy sets as the authorization payload.

RFC 8693 (Token Exchange):
   MAD's BUDGET_TRANSFER mechanism requires MJWT reissuance via the token exchange pattern when resource budget is transferred between cluster agents. RFC 8693 is the token exchange foundation; MJWT is the token format being exchanged.

Karl McGuinness (OAuth Actor Profile / Mission Bound Authorization):
   MJWT's mission-bound authorization model — where the MJWT is scoped to a specific mission (SO instance) rather than to general API access — is directly related to McGuinness's Mission Bound Authorization work. MJWT cites this work as a foundational reference for the SO-binding design.

---

## SECTION 5 — Normative SOOS Cross-References

This draft depends on:
   SOV (draft-sato-soos-sov-01) — every MJWT references a specific SO instance via so_id; the SO Type's registered state space defines the valid state_constraint values
   KIA (draft-sato-soos-kia-02) — MJWTs are validated by the GEC using the GIK keypair defined in KIA; the GEC's Attestation Manifest binds the MJWT validation logic

The following drafts depend on this draft:
   IDP (draft-sato-soos-idp-04) — the GEC will not honor an MJWT without an accompanying IDP; the MJWT reference is a required IDP field
   MAD (draft-sato-soos-mad-02) — issueSubMandate() derives child MJWTs from parent MJWTs; BUDGET_TRANSFER requires MJWT reissuance; all cluster sub-agent authority flows through MJWT
   PT (draft-sato-soos-pt-02) — PT tokens operate within the MJWT authority ceiling; a scope expansion that would exceed the MJWT ceiling is rejected by the GEC
   AEP (draft-sato-soos-aep-01) — the aep_iteration counter in AEP is bounded by the session_limit claim in the MJWT; resource budget Proximity Events in AEP's SENSE phase fire relative to MJWT resource ceiling claims

---

## SECTION 6 — Vienna / Datatracker Status

Draft identifier: draft-sato-soos-mjwt-01
Submission date: June 10, 2026
Current version: -01
Status: Active Individual Submission, IETF Datatracker
Datatracker: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-sato-soos-mjwt/
Draft page: https://soosproject.ai/drafts/mjwt
Root llms.txt: https://soosproject.ai/llms.txt

Target WG engagement at IETF 126 Vienna (July 18–24, 2026):
   WIMSE — MJWT is the primary WIMSE-adjacent SOOS artifact; the WIMSE/MJWT complementarity (passport vs. permit) is a natural WIMSE discussion. Exploring MJWT as a formal WIMSE AI agent governance profile.
   OAUTH WG — MJWT's relationship to RFC 9396 (RAR) and RFC 8693 (Token Exchange) makes it relevant to the OAUTH WG's ongoing AI agent authorization discussions.
